title: Exodus 38
subtitle: Bronze Altar and Basin; the Courtyard; Materials Census

sections.header: Verses 1—20 — The Altar of Burnt Offering and the Bronze Basin

heb.paragraph: V.1-8: The outer courtyard's altar and laver were bronze (nĕḥōshet) — not gold or silver. The progression of materials from courtyard to Holy Place to Most Holy Place (bronze → silver → gold) maps the gradient of holiness. Bronze, associated with judgment and strength (see bronze serpent, Num 21), appropriately marks the place of sacrifice and washing — the first encounters with God require dealing with sin and impurity before entry becomes possible.
The Hebrew text uses מַרְאֹת (mar'ot) for mirrors in Exodus 38:8, not מַחֲלָפוֹת

hist.historical: The bronze altar and basin were made from bronze (an alloy of copper and tin), not iron — consistent with Late Bronze Age metallurgy before the Iron Age transition (c. 1200 BC). The mirrors donated by the women who served at the entrance (v. 8) were polished bronze discs, standard in Egyptian cosmetic practice — hundreds have been found in Egyptian tombs. That women 'served' (ṣāvāʾ, a term also meaning 'to muster for service') at the sanctuary entrance suggests an organised female ministry attested nowhere else in the Pentateuch.
hist.context: The bronze basin from the women's mirrors (v.8) is one of the most unexpected details in the Tabernacle construction. These women gave their personal beauty objects — the mirrors that showed them their own faces — to make the basin where priests would wash before entering God's presence. Their sacrifice is both literal and symbolic: what serves self-reflection is given for holy service.

refs.note: Hannah's son Samuel and the women who served at the entrance to the tent of meeting — the ministry of these women continued through the period of the judges.
Women who served Jesus from their own resources — the same pattern of women's devoted practical service continuing in the NT.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Sarna notes the women's mirrors (mar'ot) given for the bronze basin represent the most personal possible contribution — objects of daily self-regard transformed into an instrument of sacred purification. The giving is sacrificial: what is intimate and personal given for what is communal and holy.

The materials census is one of the Bible's most detailed financial accounts. Alter notes total accountability — every talent of gold, every shekel of silver documented by function and location. The sacred community's bookkeeping is as careful as its worship.

Calvin: 'The women, renouncing all their vanity, cheerfully offered their mirrors for so holy a use. We are taught that all our possessions are to be consecrated to God as soon as he shows us a legitimate use for them.'

The NET note calculates: 603,550 men × 1 bekah = 100 talents + 1,775 shekels of silver. This confirms the census figures of Num 1:46 — the tabernacle's silver foundation matches the covenant community's registered membership exactly. Every registered person contributed to the foundation.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The materials census is one of the Bible's most detailed financial accounts. Alter notes total accountability — every talent of gold, every shekel of silver documented by function and location. The sacred community's bookkeeping is as careful as its worship.

Calvin: 'The women, renouncing all their vanity, cheerfully offered their mirrors for so holy a use. We are taught that all our possessions are to be consecrated to God as soon as he shows us a legitimate use for them.'

The NET note calculates: 603,550 men × 1 bekah = 100 talents + 1,775 shekels of silver. This confirms the census figures of Num 1:46 — the tabernacle's silver foundation matches the covenant community's registered membership exactly. Every registered person contributed to the foundation.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note calculates: 603,550 men × 1 bekah = 100 talents + 1,775 shekels of silver. This confirms the census figures of Num 1:46 — the tabernacle's silver foundation matches the covenant community's registered membership exactly. Every registered person contributed to the foundation.

The NET note on the bronze altar's construction discusses the acacia-wood-bronze combination. The note observes that wood alone would burn in a fire altar; bronze alone would be prohibitively heavy. The combination achieves the required properties: a structure light enough to carry but fireproof enough for sacrificial use. The engineering is covenant practicality.

MacArthur: He built the altar of burnt offering. The altar of burnt offering is the entry point of the tabernacle court. Every approach to the divine presence begins at the altar. The exclusivity of approach through atonement is structural, not arbitrary.

MacArthur: They made the bronze basin from the mirrors of the women who served at the entrance to the tent of meeting. The women's bronze mirrors -- instruments of self-regard -- are repurposed for priestly cleansing.

sections.header: Verses 21—31 — The Inventory of Materials Used

heb.gloss: silver of the census / counted ones
heb.paragraph: V.25-28: The silver from the census — 301,775 half-shekels — was cast into the 100 silver sockets (ʾădānîm) of the Tabernacle framework. Every socket of the Tabernacle's foundation was made of half a shekel of silver from a counted Israelite. The word pĕqûdîm (counted/mustered) means both "census" and "care/appointment" — to be counted by God is to be under his oversight. The foundation of the dwelling place was the counted, redeemed people.
פְּקֻדִּים (pĕqûdîm) means 'counted ones/census' but בִּקּוּרִים (biqqûrîm) means 'firstfruits', not accounting/inventory

hist.historical: The materials inventory — 29 talents and 730 shekels of gold (c. 1,000 kg), 100 talents and 1,775 shekels of silver (c. 3,400 kg), 70 talents and 2,400 shekels of bronze (c. 2,400 kg) — represents enormous wealth. By comparison, Tutankhamun's innermost coffin alone contained approximately 110 kg of gold. The silver came entirely from the half-shekel census tax, explicitly linking the sanctuary's foundation to the whole community. Ithamar son of Aaron served as the inventory accountant — establishing priestly oversight of temple finances.
hist.context: The materials census (vv.21–31) is an act of covenantal accountability — everything given is accounted for, verified, and reported. The gold, silver, and bronze are all tallied. The 100 silver talents cast into 100 bases (v.27: one talent per base, one base per person counted) is the Tabernacle's most explicit connection between the ransom silver and the community's redemption. The Tabernacle stood on 603,550 people's atonement payments.

refs.note: The silver for the bases came from the census atonement money — the half-shekel per person commanded in 30:11–16. The inventory proves that every shekel of the people's contribution was accounted for.
Numbers 7 records the tribal leaders' offerings at the tabernacle's dedication. The accounting precision here establishes the pattern: sacred gifts require transparent stewardship.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The courtyard dimensions (100 × 50 cubits) produce specific proportions. Sarna notes the sanctuary occupies exactly half the courtyard's length, situated at the western end — making the altar and laver the forward half of the sacred precinct. The spatial arrangement emphasises the altar's centrality.

The total materials census — 29 talents 730 shekels of gold, 100 talents 1,775 shekels of silver, 70 talents 2,400 shekels of bronze — represents one of the earliest financial accountings in history. Sarna notes the precision is not merely administrative but theological: sacred generosity is fully documented. What the community gave, the community can verify.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The courtyard is the final element of the tabernacle complex — the outermost boundary before the ordinary world. Alter notes the chapter concludes the construction section's movement from the most sacred interior (the Ark) outward to the public boundary (the courtyard screen).

The materials census closes the construction narrative with financial accountability. Alter notes the census is both a record of what was given and an implicit statement about the size of Israel: the silver from 603,550 men (one half-shekel each) exactly accounts for the silver total. The community's census and the building's materials audit are perfectly correlated.

Calvin on the bronze altar: 'The altar of burnt-offering was placed in the court, before the tabernacle, that the people might understand they could only approach God through the medium of sacrifice.' The altar's position teaches the structure of approach.

Calvin: Moses records the exact inventory so that no one could accuse the craftsmen of misappropriation. Sacred work requires sacred accountability — the covenant community's worship is also its transparent financial record. Nothing is hidden from God or from the community.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the materials census confirms it as a genuine financial accounting. The internal consistency — 603,550 men × half-shekel = exactly 100 talents + 1,775 shekels — demonstrates authentic record-keeping, not theological invention.

The NET note on the 100 talents of silver from the half-shekel tax traces the calculation: 603,550 men × 1/2 shekel each = 100 talents and 1,775 shekels. The note confirms the arithmetic is exact — the census count and the materials inventory check each other. The mathematical precision is the narrative's demonstration of complete accountability.

MacArthur: These are the amounts of the materials used for the tabernacle, which were recorded at Moses's command. The audited record of materials is theological testimony: sacred work requires sacred accountability.

MacArthur: The financial accounting of the tabernacle materials is not bureaucratic formality but theological statement. What the community gave to God is carefully inventoried. The total — 29 talents of gold, 100 talents of silver, 70 talents of bronze — represents the community's collective act of worship. Worship has a measurable cost, and the cost is recorded.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: Aaron↗ People
ppl.text: Aaron, Moses’s brother, is consecrated as Israel’s first high priest in Lev 8–9. He bears the names of the twelve tribes on his breastplate before God and enters the Most Holy Place once a year on Yom Kippur (Lev 16). His failures — the golden calf (Exod 32), challenging Moses’s authority (Num 12), complicity at Meribah (Num 20) — make him the paradigm of the imperfect priest who prefigures a better high priest (Heb 7:26–28).

ppl.name: Pharaoh
ppl.text: Pharaoh is a key figure in Scripture Walkthrough.

ppl.name: LORD
ppl.text: LORD is a key figure in Scripture Walkthrough.

ppl.name: Miriam↗ People
ppl.role: Prophetess and worship leader; Moses’s sister
ppl.text: Miriam leads Israel’s women in worship after the Red Sea crossing (Exod 15:20) and is called a prophetess. In Num 12 she challenges Moses’s unique prophetic authority alongside Aaron and is struck with ṣāraʿat — only to be healed after Moses’s seven-word intercession. She dies at Kadesh (Num 20:1), her passing noted in two terse Hebrew words before the water crisis.

ppl.name: Joshua↗ People
ppl.role: Moses’s military commander and designated successor
ppl.text: Joshua son of Nun serves as Moses’s lieutenant throughout the wilderness period — commanding at Rephidim (Exod 17), ascending Sinai with Moses (Exod 24), guarding the tent of meeting. In Num 13–14 he stands with Caleb as one of two faithful spies. He is formally commissioned in Num 27 through Moses’s hand-laying and inherits the leadership role that Moses cannot fulfil past the Jordan.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationESV"A beka a head (that is, half a shekel, by the shekel of the sanctuary), for everyone who was listed in the census, from twenty years old and upward, for 603,550 men."NIV"One beka per person, that is, half a shekel, according to the sanctuary shekel, from everyone who had crossed over to those already counted, from twenty years old or more, a total of 603,550 men."

src.title: 603,550 Men
src.quote: The census number here matches Numbers 1:46. The consistency across Exodus 38 and Numbers 1 is significant. Various interpretations exist for the large number: literal; ʾelep as "unit" rather than "thousand" (giving a much smaller total); symbolic for a great multitude.
src.note: The number is consistent across texts; its interpretation remains debated.

src.title: Materials Accounting
src.quote: The detailed materials census has parallels in ancient building accounts — Egyptian and Mesopotamian building inscriptions often include detailed accounts of materials used. The Tabernacle census fits within this ancient tradition of temple accounting.
src.note: Accountability for sacred materials was a universal ancient Near Eastern practice.

rec.text: The census of 603,550 men reappears in Numbers 1 — confirming the connection between the Tabernacle's silver bases and the whole community's counted, ransomed lives.

The materials census of Exod 38 became the model for covenantal financial accountability — the principle that God's resources must be carefully accounted for, as exemplified in Nehemiah's rebuilding (Neh 7:70–73) and the NT church's collection for Jerusalem (2 Cor 8:20–21).

rows.label: Materials Inventory: Sacred Accountability
rows.text: This is the inventory of the tabernacle: recorded by Moses at the command of the LORD. The precise financial accounting -- every talent, shekel, and gerah -- establishes that sacred work requires sacred accountability. What the community gave, the community can verify.

rows.label: Bronze Laver: The Mirror of the Women
rows.text: He made the bronze basin and its stand from the mirrors of the women who served at the entrance to the tent of meeting. The women's bronze mirrors -- instruments of self-regard -- become the instrument of priestly purification.

hebtext.note: V.1-8: The outer courtyard's altar and laver were bronze (nĕḥōshet) — not gold or silver. The progression of materials from courtyard to Holy Place to Most Holy Place (bronze → silver → gold) maps the gradient of holiness. Bronze, associated with judgment and strength (see bronze serpent, Num 21), appropriately marks the place of sacrifice and washing — the first encounters with God require dealing with sin and impurity before entry becomes possible.
מַחֲלָפוֹת (maḥalāpôt) = mirrors (specifically the bronze mirrors of the serving women)

hebtext.gloss: silver of the census / counted ones
hebtext.note: V.25-28: The silver from the census — 301,775 half-shekels — was cast into the 100 silver sockets (ʾădānîm) of the Tabernacle framework. Every socket of the Tabernacle's foundation was made of half a shekel of silver from a counted Israelite. The word pĕqûdîm (counted/mustered) means both "census" and "care/appointment" — to be counted by God is to be under his oversight. The foundation of the dwelling place was the counted, redeemed people.

V.21: "These are the amounts of the materials used for the tabernacle, the tabernacle of the Testimony, which were recorded (meforshim) at Moses's command." The careful accounting of all materials used — down to the last talent of bronze — reflects the accountability principle of public trust. Moses gave full transparency: the community's offering was faithfully used. The stewardship of sacred gifts is subject to open accounting.

thread.text: Hannah's son Samuel and the women who served at the entrance to the tent of meeting — the ministry of these women continued through the period of the judges.
Women who served Jesus from their own resources — the same pattern of women's devoted practical service continuing in the NT.

tx.title: Metal inventory — numerical precision
tx.content: MT provides exact quantities of gold, silver, and bronze used in the tabernacle. The LXX preserves these numbers with only minor differences in certain manuscripts. The SP agrees with MT. The totals are internally consistent within MT, which is remarkable for ancient numerical transmission.
tx.note: The internal consistency of the metal totals across different calculations is a textual control — if any number had been corrupted, the totals would not add up. This built-in check preserved the figures.

debate.title: The Materials Census: Historical Record or Theological Testimony?

positions.name: Historical record
positions.argument: The census is a genuine financial record consistent with ancient Near Eastern temple building records. Egyptian and Mesopotamian records routinely documented materials used.

positions.name: Theological idealization
positions.argument: The numbers are too precise to be genuine records — a theological statement about perfect obedience, not an accountant's ledger.

coaching.tip: The bronze altar and basin (vv. 1-8). Women's mirrors become the basin—vanity repurposed for purification. What once reflected faces now cleanses hands. Transformation redeems ordinary objects.

The material inventory: nearly a ton of gold, three tons of silver, two and a half tons of bronze (vv. 24-29). Precise accounting. God's house is built with auditable generosity. Stewardship requires records.

prayer_prompt: As you consider the careful accounting of every talent and shekel used in the tabernacle, consider asking God whether you steward the resources He has given you with the same transparency and accountability — and whether anything is unaccounted for.
